coredata.dc:title: Zn-doped carbon dots-based versatile bioanalytical probe for precise estimation of antioxidant activity index of multiple samples via fenton chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Sohail, Muhammad
Xie, Bin
Li, Bingzhi
Huang, He
coredata.dc:description: The significance of antioxidant activity determination in health sciences and food technology has become a hot research area as it is directly related to oxidative stress which is affiliated with several diseases and food deterioration. Herein, we fabricated a Zn-CDs-based (Zn-doped carbon dots-based) bioanalytical probe for the estimation of antioxidant activity index (AAI) to cope with the truncated robustness, high interference effect in complex samples, photo-bleaching, and low stability of the traditional techniques. Besides, the novel AAI standardized the presentation of the results by considering the transducer, Zn-CDs, concentration as reported results vary diversely for the same sample. In principle, free radicals quenched the fluorescence of the probe via inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. The presence of antioxidants hindered the fluorescence quenching by sequestering the free radicals from the reaction. The designed nanoreagent was validated for its applications using ascorbic acid, human serum, topical cosmetic formulation, and tomato juice samples. The bioanalytical probe demonstrated precise linearity, high robustness, low interference effect, and analytical equivalence when compared to the DPPH free radical assay. Hence, it can be used for the diagnosis and follow-up of oxidative stress-related ailments, developing and characterizing cosmeceuticals, analysis of antioxidant-containing formulations, and assessing and controlling the quality of food products. It is the first report to explore the antioxidant feature of Zn-CDs and its advantage for the estimation of AAI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioanalytical probe
Zn-CDs
Antioxidant activity
Human serum
Cosmeceuticals
Food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Schematic design of the bioanalytical probe
Reaction parameter optimization and statistical modeling
Estimation of AAI of real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Research of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China

Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20200718 to B.Li), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China ( 20KJB350010 to B.Li), Jiangsu Innovation and Entrepreneurship Doctoral Program to X.Zhang, and Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Project to X.Zhang.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of Zn-CDs: (a) HR-TEM image at 10 nm scale, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) Fluorescence (FL) excitation and emission spectra, (d) UV-Vis absorption spectrum.

(a) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum of Zn-CDs overlapped the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the quencher, mixture of Fe2+ and H2O2. (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra of the Zn-CDs before and after the addition of the quencher remained unchanged. (c) The Stern-Volmer plot of the Zn-CDs shifted upwards as the temperature was raised from 20 °C to 35 °C. “F0” and “F” denoted the fluorescence intensities of Zn-CDs before and after the addition of the quencher, respectively. (d) FL lifetime spectra of the Zn-CDs before and after the addition of the quencher changed.

(a) Profile plot for the estimation of interaction effect of pH and Fe2+ concentration. (b) Residual plots for the enhancement of fluorescence intensity with respect to reaction parameters based on designed statistical model.

(a) Increase in fluorescence intensity of the nanoreagent with the gradual 1 µg/mL addition of ascorbic acid a-j (0 µg/mL and 2–10 µg/mL). The top right corner of (a) presents a visual display of the nanoprobe under UV light, where a, b, and c-k denote Zn-CDs, the probe with 0 µg/mL and 2–10 µg/mL with 1 µg/mL increments of ascorbic acid, respectively. (b) (F0-F)/F0 increased linearly with the concentration of ascorbic acid in the range of 2–10 µg/mL. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the bioanalytical probe before and after the addition of the ascorbic acid, respectively.
Variation in output signals of the bioanalytical probe in the presence of various interfering agents along with the target.

Schematic design of the nanoprobe. Fenton chemistry generated OH. and HOO. free radicals that efficiently quenched the fluorescence of the Zn-CDs. The presence of analyte sequestered the free radicals from the system leading to the inhibition of CDs’ quenching.
Validation of the procedure by the parameters of accuracy (RE %) and precision (RSD %). Ascorbic acid (µg/mL) was used as a standard antioxidant.
Effect of Zn-CDs’ concentration on IC50 and AAI of standard antioxidant (ascorbic acid). The experiments were performed in triplicates.
Antioxidant activity index (AAI) and IC50 values of the human serum, topical cream, and tomato juice samples determined by the Zn-CDs-based fluorescent bioanalytical probe.
AAI values of the serum, topical cream, and tomato juice determined by the designed bioanalytical probe and DPPH assay.

head.ce:title: Zn-doped carbon dots-based versatile bioanalytical probe for precise estimation of antioxidant activity index of multiple samples via fenton chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Nanjing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pathology, Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, 87 Xiangya Road, Changsha, Hunan, 410078 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pathology, Xiangya Hospital, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pathology, Xiangya Hospital, Central South University, 87 Xiangya Road, Changsha, Hunan, 410078 China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: M. Sohail and B. Xie contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The significance of antioxidant activity determination in health sciences and food technology has become a hot research area as it is directly related to oxidative stress which is affiliated with several diseases and food deterioration. Herein, we fabricated a Zn-CDs-based (Zn-doped carbon dots-based) bioanalytical probe for the estimation of antioxidant activity index (AAI) to cope with the truncated robustness, high interference effect in complex samples, photo-bleaching, and low stability of the traditional techniques. Besides, the novel AAI standardized the presentation of the results by considering the transducer, Zn-CDs, concentration as reported results vary diversely for the same sample. In principle, free radicals quenched the fluorescence of the probe via inner filter effect and dynamic quenching. The presence of antioxidants hindered the fluorescence quenching by sequestering the free radicals from the reaction. The designed nanoreagent was validated for its applications using ascorbic acid, human serum, topical cosmetic formulation, and tomato juice samples. The bioanalytical probe demonstrated precise linearity, high robustness, low interference effect, and analytical equivalence when compared to the DPPH free radical assay. Hence, it can be used for the diagnosis and follow-up of oxidative stress-related ailments, developing and characterizing cosmeceuticals, analysis of antioxidant-containing formulations, and assessing and controlling the quality of food products. It is the first report to explore the antioxidant feature of Zn-CDs and its advantage for the estimation of AAI.

ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidants are the entities that can prevent or hinder the oxidation of vital biomolecules by restraining the initiation and/or propagation of the free radical chain reactions. Their features assist in neutralizing the free radicals, sequestering oxygen, or degrading peroxides to protect against oxidative damage [1,2]. There exists a balance between the levels of oxidants and antioxidants in an organism. The total antioxidant activity (TAA) refers to the resultant potential of the whole sample to sequester free radicals [3]. The term "oxidative stress" is affiliated with the low levels of antioxidants, and is associated with various human diseases, including cancer, cardiovascular diseases, Alzheimer’s disease, liver injury, sleep bruxism, skin disorders, and aging [4–8]. The oxidant/antioxidant balance is retrieved in a variety of ways, such as food supplementation, drugs, and cosmetic preparations.

Besides, antioxidant capacity is one of the parameters to assess the quality of the food products and control various determinants during the production of plant products to enhance the desired yield [9–11]. Moreover, the development of cosmeceuticals requires the estimation of the antioxidant activities of the active ingredients and the final product [12–15]. Natural ingredient-containing products are gaining popularity due to fewer side effects and better outcomes [16]. These formulations own a major section of the scope of antioxidants. Hence, determination of antioxidant capacity is a valuable tool for the diagnosis of oxidative stress-related ailments and their follow-up, quality assurance testing of drugs and food supplements containing antioxidants, verification of the quality of plant products, validation of cosmeceuticals for intended use, and development of antioxidant-based formulations.

Researchers have developed a pool of analytical systems for antioxidant activity determination [17]. These platforms vary in their mechanisms and output signals [18]. The most of the reported methods use the principles of colorimetry [19–22], fluorescence spectrometry [21,23], chemiluminescence [24], voltammetry [25,26], photoelectrochemical [27,28], electrochemical [29–34], biamperometry [35], potentiometric [36], gas chromatography [37], and high performance liquid chromatography [38]. Spectrometry is the most widely used method which is based on fluorescent or colorimetric dyes [39]. These techniques offer various advantages but there is no standard commercial technique to monitor the antioxidant potential of multiple samples, especially the complex biological samples [40]. The reported analytical methods have been mostly validated for a single sample or a group of related antioxidants [41]. Moreover, the presentation of results varied widely [3,42]. The drawbacks of the already available methods include photo-bleaching of the dye, low stability, narrow application spectrum, poor performance in the case of complex samples, and variation in the presentation of results [21,41,43]. The fluorescent carbon dots-based biosensors are expected to be state-of-the-art biosensors due to unique fluorescent features, photo-thermal stability, nano size, and peculiar surface features of the carbon dots [44–46]. Their versatile applications in biosensor design have made them a point of focus for researchers in fabricating commercial analytical platforms.

This project is aimed to design a Zn-CDs-based bioanalytical probe using Fenton reaction for the estimation of antioxidant activity index of a broad range of samples. The Zn-CDs own antioxidant features [47] and offer reduced interference effects [48] due to surface functional groups and doping, respectively. It is the first report to highlight the antioxidant feature of Zn-CDs for the estimation of antioxidant activity index that is a valuable addition to their scope. Besides, stable fluorescence, resistance to photo-bleaching, and long shelf-life are some of the features which precisely overcame the lacking of the reported dyes-based methods [49]. Briefly, the resultant of these features offered a robust, reliable, cost-effective, and versatile nanoreagent for the said purpose. We believe that this novelty will be valuable for the diagnosis and follow-up of oxidative stress-related ailments, developing and characterizing cosmeceutical formulations, analysis of antioxidant-containing dosage forms, and assessing and controlling the quality of the food products owing antioxidants. Moreover, a novel index for the estimation of antioxidant potential named “Antioxidant activity index (AAI)” has been introduced based on the transducer, Zn-CDs, concentration. This second novelty will be useful in the interpretation of the results in a standard form. We used the human serum, cosmetic, and food samples to validate the versatility of the bioanalytical probe for a broad range of antioxidants.

Trisodium citrate dihydrate, acetic acid, sodium acetate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium bicarbonate, sodium hydroxide, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, magnesium chloride, ferric chloride, ammonium bicarbonate, copper sulphate, zinc chloride, ferrous chloride, hydrogen peroxide, urea, uric acid, and albumin were purchased from Sinopharm Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl was from Macklin reagent (Shanghai, China), while quinine sulphate and ascorbic acid were from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). A Millipore Milli-Q system (MA, U.S.A.) was used for the preparation of ultrapure water. All the reagents used in this experiment were of analytical grade.

Biotek Synergy H1 hybrid multi-mode microplate reader BioTek (VT, USA) was used for fluorescence and UV-Visible experiments, Thermo Fisher Nicolet Is5 FTIR (MA, USA) for Fourier transform infrared spectrum, FLS1000 spectrometer Edinburgh (Livingston, UK) for fluorescence lifetime measurements, and Thermo Fisher FEI Talos F200S Super-X HR-TEM (MA, USA) for surface features of the particles.
IBM SPSS 20, Origin lab pro 8.5, graph-pad prism 5, design expert 11, endnote X7, and MS excel 13.

Human serum samples were obtained from the center of disease control and prevention (CDC) Jiangsu, P.R. China. The cosmetic topical cream sample was obtained from the local supermarket. 1 g of the cream was extracted with 3 mL of 70% ethanol in water and filtered. The resultant filtrate was used as a sample. The tomato, Solanum lycopersicum, was purchased from the local market. The fruit without peal was crushed and squeezed to get the juice. The juice was filtered and the filtrate was used as a sample.

Zn-CDs were synthesized via a modified bottom-up hydrothermal approach. Briefly, 0.20 g ZnCl2 was mixed with 0.88 g trisodium citrate dihydrate in 30 mL of water. The precursors, ZnCl2 and trisodium citrate dihydrate served as sources of zinc and carbon backbone, respectively. The reaction mixture was poured into a 50 mL stainless-steel equipped Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the completion of the reaction, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 µm syringe filter. The filtrate was concentrated to 1 mL using a rotary evaporator operated at 40 °C, and purified using a 3000D dialysis membrane for 48 h and water as a medium. Finally, the product was lyophilized and ground to powder form for further experimentation.

Zn-CDs were characterized for their optical and surface features using various techniques. The fluorescence emission and excitation wavelengths, λEM and λEX, were determined by using the fluorescence spectrum scan mode of the microplate reader. The quantum yield was measured by well-known quinine sulphate method where the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the standard was 54% [50]. The absorbance and fluorescence intensities of the standard and sample solutions, with absorbance intensities < 0.1, were recorded on a microplate reader, and the absolute PLQY of the Zn-CDs was calculated. The solution of the Zn-CDs was prepared in deionized water while that of standard was prepared in 0.1 MH2SO4.

The fluorescence and absorption spectrum, functional group confirmation, and morphological traits were recorded using hybrid multimode microplate reader, FTIR spectrometer, and HR-TEM, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime spectrum was recorded with the FLS1000 spectrometer.

Fenton chemistry was used to develop the bioprobe which involved the reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+ to generate the hydroxyl free radicals [51]. The free radicals significantly quenched the fluorescence of the Zn-CDs. The presence of an antioxidant sequestered the free radicals generated during the Fenton reaction resulting in enhanced fluorescence signals. The normalized fluorescence was calibrated against the concentration of the ascorbic acid, the standard. Finally, the AAI of the standard and sample solutions was determined by using Eq. 1.
(1) AAI = c (Zn-CDs) / IC50

In Eq. 1, the c (Zn-CDs) expressed the concentration of Zn-CDs (µg/mL) while IC50 (µg/mL) indicated the concentration of the antioxidant required to inhibit the fluorescence quenching of the Zn-CDs by 50%. IC50 can be determined by plotting the calibration curve between the percentage of the inhibited fluorescence quenching of Zn-CDs and the concentration of the antioxidant. The concentration of the antioxidants in the sample was estimated by using ascorbic acid equivalent (AAE) according to Eq. 2. The samples were diluted to proper concentrations for the determination of IC50. The concentration of ascorbic acid and AAE were expressed as µg/mL in Eq. 2.
(2) AAE = [(FLIblank - FLIsample) / (FLIblank - FLIascorbic acid)] × c (ascorbic acid)

Here, FLIblank, FLIsample and FLIascorbic acid indicated the fluorescence intensities of blank (without antioxidant), sample and ascorbic acid, respectively. The c (ascorbic acid) denoted the concentration of ascorbic acid (µg/mL). Besides, the components of the bioanalytical probe including concentrations of the H2O2, Fe2+, and Zn-CDs, buffer pH, buffer concentration, reaction pH, and reaction time, were optimized by using the statistical approach.

The solution of Fe2+ (4 mM, 50 µL) and the McIlvaine buffer of pH 3 (25 mM, 200 µL) were added to H2O2 (40 mM, 50 µL). Meanwhile, 50 µL of the antioxidant/sample and deionized water were added to the sample and blank test tubes, respectively. The mixtures were incubated at 20 °C for 1 h. After the said duration, 200 µL of 5 µg/mL Zn-CDs were added to each reaction mixture and incubated at 20 °C for 2 h. The fluorescence intensities of the blank, F0, and the sample, F, were recorded. The normalized change in fluorescence intensity, (F-F0)/ F0, as percentage versus concentration of the antioxidant, were plotted to find IC50 and AAI. The same procedure was adopted for the estimation of AAI of the real samples. Moreover, all the solutions were freshly prepared using ultrapure water.

Various determinants such as quantities of reactants, temperature, time and pH etc., control a reaction and assist in optimizing a system for the maximum output. The highest yield of a reaction expresses its optimum level where all parameters have their particular values. These factors may affect the system directly, indirectly, individually, or in combination. Besides, some essential constituents may not affect the output significantly. Varying levels of reaction determinants were investigated and optimized.

In this experiment, normalized fluorescence intensity change was the output while concentrations of Fe2+, H2O2, and Zn-CDs, buffer pH and its concentration, reaction temperature and time were the factors. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) with multiple comparisons at 5% level of significance was used to select the optimum levels of the reaction determinants. “FLI” and “FLIR” indicated “fluorescence intensity” and “fluorescence intensity restoration”, respectively.

The nanoparticles were characterized for their surface morphology, optical features, and presence of functional groups. Fig. 1 presented different traits of the Zn-CDs. The nanoparticles were roughly spherical with a diameter less than 10 nm as revealed in the HR-TEM image, Fig. 1a. Fig. 1a was captured at a 10 nm scale.

FTIR spectrum of the Zn-CDs confirmed the presence of various functional groups, Fig. 1b. The remarkable peaks were at 3441 cm−1, 1587 cm−1, 1397 cm−1, 1251 cm−1, and 1088 cm−1, and these were the representative peaks for O-H stretching vibrations, CO stretching vibrations/asymmetric vibrations of COO-, C-H bending and stretching vibrations/symmetric vibrations of COO-, O-H bending vibrations, and C-C/C-O stretching vibrations, respectively [52–56]. In short, the peaks in the functional group sector, 1500–4000 cm−1 justified the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, while peaks in the fingerprint region, 400–1500 cm−1, might be due to the complex molecular deformities. The described functional groups are attributed to the high water solubility of the Zn-CDs. Fig. 1c and 1d presented the optical traits of the Zn-CDs in terms of fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis absorption spectrum. The sharp fluorescence excitation and emission peaks were observed at 340 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The absorption spectrum in Fig. 1d exhibited radiation absorbance in the region 300–600 nm that was strong at the start, presented a peak at about 340 nm, and decreased gradually with the wavelength. The specific absorption pattern is an explanation of π-π * and n-π * transitions of CC and CO, respectively. Moreover, the PLQY of the Zn-CDs was 33.7%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Schematic design of the bioanalytical probe
ce:section.ce:para: The basic design and working principle of the nanoreagent have been demonstrated in Scheme 1.

Two principles, the redox reaction, and fluorescence quenching were involved in the concept design. The first step entailed the Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2. The said reaction resulted in the oxidized form of iron (Fe3+), hydroxyl ion (OH-), and hydroxyl free radical (OH.). The reduction of the generated Fe3+ converted H2O2 into a hydrogen ion (H+) and hydroperoxyl free radical (HOO.). In the second step, the free radicals produced during the Fenton reaction significantly quenched the fluorescence of the Zn-CDs. The presence of the analyte sequestered the generated free radicals and got oxidized, resulting in inhibition of the fluorescence quenching. In short, the target analyte indirectly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the Zn-CDs. The described “inhibition of fluorescence quenching” principle along with the antioxidant feature of Zn-CDs is used for the first time to estimate the antioxidant activity index of multiple complex samples.

Besides, the experiments were conducted to explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. Fig. 2 demonstrated the results of the said experiments. It was observed that the fluorescence emission spectrum of the fluorophore, Zn-CDs, overlapped the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the quencher, mixture of Fe2+ and H2O2, Fig. 2a. Furthermore, there was no change in the absorption spectra of the Zn-CDs before and after the addition of quencher when scanned in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm, Fig. 2b. The Stern-Volmer plot presented a linear trend where F0/F increased gradually with the concentration of the quencher. “F0” and “F” denoted the fluorescence intensities of Zn-CDs before and after the addition of the quencher, respectively. The concentration of Fe2+ was considered for the Stern-Volmer plot as it acted as a limiting reagent in the Fenton reaction. The reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 was completed according to the optimized procedure. The reaction mixture was diluted to get the required concentrations of the limiting reactant, and the resulting dilutions were used as the quencher. Moreover, static and dynamic quenching mechanisms were differentiated by examining the effect of temperature on the Stern-Volmer plot and fluorescence lifetime measurements of the Zn-CDs before and after the addition of the quencher. A clear upward shift of the Stern-Volmer plot was observed as the reaction temperature was elevated from 20 °C to 35 °C, Fig. 2c. The fluorescence lifetime of the Zn-CDs was changed as they interacted with the quencher, i.e. Ʈ0/Ʈ ‡ 1. “Ʈ0” and “Ʈ” indicated the fluorescence lifetimes of Zn-CDs before and after the quenching, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime spectra have been presented in Fig. 2d.

Based on the literature, it was concluded from the findings presented in Fig. 2 that the inner filter effect and dynamic quenching were the possible mechanisms involved to sequester the fluorescence of the Zn-CDs [57,58]. The demonstrated dual quenching mechanism is a unique feature of the Zn-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction parameter optimization and statistical modeling
ce:section.ce:para:
All the reaction determinants were assessed to determine their impact on the fluorescence enhancement, the output of the reaction. A statistical model was designed for the said purpose to screen out the parameters significantly affecting the outcome independently or in combination. Analysis of variance with multiple comparisons and interaction effects was performed via posthoc test, LSD, and Tukey’s analyses. Besides, regression analysis was performed to estimate the function equation and pattern of variation. Fig. S1 described the effect of reaction determinants on FLI. The variation of the output at various levels of each parameter and the optimum level has been described in the section “Reaction parameter optimization” of the supplementary data. A polynomial regression model of degree 3 was demonstrated by the data when a 35-run design was applied. The statistical analysis resulted in Eq. 3.
(3) Zn-CDs’ FLI (%) = - 1·0564·102 + 4·9257·10−2β1 3 - 1·2603·100β1 2 + 8·2089·100β1 - 1·8571·10−4β2 3 + 1·1786·10−2β2 2 - 7·1857·10−2β2 + 2·3457·10−2β3 3 - 5·0370·10−1β3 2 + 2·4376·100β3 + 6·8761·10−1β4 3 - 1·0104·101β4 2 + 4·4656·101β4 - 9·8429·10−3β5 2 + 2·5430·10−1β5 + 3·7714·10−3β6 3 - 3·7386·10−1β6 2 + 1·1736·101β6 + 2·8714·10−3β7 2 - 1·9987·10−1β7

In Eq. 3, − 1.0564.102 = Intercept, β1 = Conc. of Fe2+ (mM), β2 = Conc. of H2O2 (mM), β3 = Conc. of Zn-CDs (µg/mL), β4 = pH value, β5 = Incubation temperature (°C), β6 = Reaction temperature (°C), β7 = Reaction time (min). The predicted response was denoted by FLIR (%). The statistical model was validated using parallel profile plots and residual plots as shown in Fig. 3.

Fig. 3a illustrated the parallel profile plot to check the estimation of combined effect of Fe2+ concentration and pH. These results validated the results of the combination model of ANOVA where p-values were > 0.05. All the 28 combinations of parameters demonstrated similar profile plots and ANOVA results. It was concluded that no significant difference in the fluorescence intensity change was there due to combined effect of reaction parameters. A polynomial regression model of degree 3 was demonstrated by the data when 35-run design was applied. The statistical analysis resulted in Eq. 3.

The model was validated using residual plots, Fig. 3b. The graphs between observed (actual), predicted (estimated by the model), and standard residual values for fluorescence intensity enhancement % were plotted. It was noted that observed versus predicted plots were linear lines with regular increments, while standard residual versus actual and predicted plots were nearly the straight horizontal or vertical lines. The described pattern of the plots validated the statistical model.
The designed bioanalytical probe was validated using standard validation parameters, i.e. linear response plot, sensitivity, accuracy and precision, and robustness.

Fig. 4 illustrated the change in fluorescence intensity of the probe on the addition of ascorbic acid and their linear relationship. The addition of ascorbic acid to the probe resulted in fluorescence enhancement recognizable with the naked eye under UV light, Fig. 4a. The nanoreagent demonstrated a linear relationship between the normalized fluorescence intensity change, (F0-F)/F0, and ascorbic acid concentration in the range of 2–10 µg/mL, Fig. 4b. “F0” and “F” denoted fluorescence intensities of the probe before and after the addition of ascorbic. Absolute values of the fluorescence intensity change were used during calculations. The linear equation for the demonstrated phenomenon was y = 0.1005x - 0.0026 with an R2 value of 0.9989. Furthermore, the principle of “3σ/slope” was used for the determination of limit of detection (LOD), which was found to be 0.210 µg/mL. The linear range and LOD justified that the nanoprobe was applicable with high sensitivity for the estimation of the antioxidant activity index of various samples.

The bioanalytical probe was validated for reproducibility via intraday and interday experiment design. Table 1 displayed the results of the said experiment along with the accuracy, relative error (RE) %, and precision, relative standard deviation (RSD) %.

Relative errors (RE) relative standard deviations (RSD) for all the measurements were − 0.80–1.92% and ≤ 1.69%, respectively. These findings revealed the reproducibility and robustness of the designed nanoprobe in terms of precision and accuracy. Hence, it conformed to the validation and can be used for real applications.

Moreover, the nanoprobe was applied to find the effect of the transducer (Zn-CDs) concentration on any deviation in the antioxidant activity index (AAI). Table 2 presented the findings of the said experiment.

It is clear from Table 2 that the IC50 value varied significantly with the concentration of the Zn-CDs, p-value < 0.05, while no significant variation was observed in the case of AAI, p-value > 0.05. Moreover, RSD% of the IC50 was much higher, 19.5%, like that of the AAI, 3.14%. Therefore, the use of AAI for the comparison of antioxidant potential is more meaningful instead of IC50. Besides, the average value of R2 (coefficient of determination) for linear plots was 0.9989.

Various biomolecules and metal ions may affect the Fenton reaction or fluorescence of the Zn-CDs or both. In any case, the output of the nanoreagent may experience a significant change and must be validated for selectivity. For the said purpose, various interfering species including common metal ions were added as solutions to the probe in the presence of the standard antioxidant, and variation in response was measured. Fig. 5 elaborated the results of the experiment.

It was found that the variation in the fluorescence change was ≤ 4.92% on the addition of various entities which may interfere in the working of the probe. These changes were not statistically significant as all the p-values were > 0.05. Additionally, the concentrations of the interfering species were much higher than the upper limits of the normal ranges found in blood and other samples. Briefly, common ions affected the fluorescence of the Zn-CDs, while other ingredients interfered in the Fenton reaction leading to a variation in the free radical generation. All of these findings proved the specificity of the probe for the AAI estimation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of AAI of real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The designed bioanalytical probe was applied for the estimation of AAI of the real samples. In the first step, ascorbic acid equivalents (AAE) of the samples were determined and dilutions were prepared accordingly to find the IC50 values. The IC50 indicated the concentration of the sample, in terms of AAE, required for the inhibition of half of the free radicals. In short, the IC50 value was the concentration where 50% response was recorded. To determine AAE, 5 µg/mL solution of the ascorbic was used as the standard antioxidant which resulted in the AAI of 1.02 µg/mL. Table 3 displayed the values of various parameters of the samples, i.e. AAE, R2 of the linear plot, IC50, AAI, and RSD %. Each sample was evaluated using three linear plots based on three variable concentrations of samples. Moreover, all the experiments were performed in triplicates and individual findings indicated the average values.

It is clear from Table 3 that the linear plots were with the acceptable R2 values. Furthermore, the RSD % values in all measurements were ≤ 1.61. These results reflected that the probe proved stable and suitable for the real sample analysis. It also narrated that the various concentrations of samples resulted in almost similar linear plots producing comparable IC50 and AAI values with low RSD %.

The nanoreagent was further validated by comparing the results with the findings of the well-known DPPH free radical method. The reported procedure was adapted for the said purpose [59]. Table 4 presented the AAI values of the serum, topical cream, and tomato juice determined by the fabricated bioanalytical probe and the DPPH method.

Based on the findings presented in Table 4, it was concluded that the designed bioanalytical probe exhibited equivalent results when compared to the DPPH method as relative deviation % values were ≤ 4.79%. Therefore, the designed probe is a stable and cost-effective alternate to the DPPH method.

A novel Zn-CDs- and Fenton reaction-based bioanalytical probe was fabricated in this project. The antioxidant feature of the Zn-CDs was explored and precisely used to estimate the antioxidant index (AAI) of multiple samples for the first time. The described trait of Zn-CDs is a valuable addition to their scope and can be used to develop the products requiring stable and cost-effective antioxidants for their endurance, such as long-life durable rubber and pharmaceuticals. Besides, the innate resistance to interference effect due to doping, high photo-thermal stability, gradual linear response with the antioxidant concentration, low-cost, and facile procedure demonstrated the designed nanoprobe as a preferred option over traditional techniques. The results narrated the robustness and versatility of the probe for real applications in oxidative stress-related diseases, cosmeceutical development, and food safety. Moreover, the literature evidenced the diversity in the results of the same sample due to the lack of a standard interpretation protocol. Most of the investigators described the antioxidant activity as inhibition percentage or IC50 which is significantly affected by the transducer concentration. Along with the nanoreagent design, a novel AAI was also introduced by considering the concentration of the transducer, Zn-CDs, during calculations. It was statistically proved that the AAI did not differ significantly at various concentrations of the transducer, while IC50 varied significantly. It is recommended to use the AAI as an antioxidant activity parameter instead of other factors to render consistent results in the literature.

Muhammad Sohail: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Bin Xie: Resources, Validation, Methodology. Bingzhi Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. He Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20200718 to B.Li), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (20KJB350010 to B.Li), Jiangsu Innovation and Entrepreneurship Doctoral Program to X.Zhang, and Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Project to X.Zhang.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131558.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: van Dongen, J.E., J.T.W. Berendsen, R.D.M. Steenbergen, R.M.F. Wolthuis, J.C.T. Eijkel, and L.I. Segerink, Point-of-care crispr/cas nucleic acid detection: Recent advances, challenges and opportunities, Biosens. Bioelectron. 166(2020) 17. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2020.112445
Lakowicz, J.R., Principles of fluorescence spectroscopy. 2013: Springer Science & Business Media.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: a review on mechanisms and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zu, F.L., F.Y. Yan, Z.J. Bai, J.X. Xu, Y.Y. Wang, Y.C. Huang, and X.G. Zhou, The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: A review on mechanisms and applications, Microchim. Acta. 184(7) (2017) 1899–1914. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604–017-2318–9

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: N.B. Gaber, S.I. El-Dahy , E.A. Shalaby , Comparison of abts, dpph, permanganate, and methylene blue assays for determining antioxidant potential of successive extracts from pomegranate and guava residues, Biomass Convers. Biorefinery. 10. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13399–021-01386–0.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Muhammad Sohail is Post-doc fellow at school of life sciences, Nanjing normal university, China. He is working on biosensor design, pharmaceutical analysis, nanomaterial synthesis, and dosage form designs.
Bin Xie is a senior pathologist at department of pathology, Xiangya hospital, central south university, China. He is working on fabricating diagnostic assays for human diseases.

Bingzhi Liis an assistant professor at school of food science and pharmaceutical engineering, Nanjing normal university, China. He is working on developing analytical sensors using novel nanomaterials for biosensing and pharmaceutical analysis.

He Huang is a professor and dean at school of food science and pharmaceutical engineering, Nanjing normal university, China. He is working on microbial biotechnology, analytical procedure design, and biosensor fabrication.
